title: Acute heart failure

infobox.value: (**ADHF**) is a sudden worsening of the signs and symptoms of [heart failure](/wiki/Heart_failure "Heart failure"), which typically includes difficulty breathing ([dyspnea](/wiki/Dyspnea "Dyspnea")), [leg or feet swelling](/wiki/Edema "Edema"), and [fatigue](/wiki/Fatigue_(medical) "Fatigue (medical)").[[1]](#cite_note-1) ADHF is a common and potentially serious cause of [acute respiratory distress](/wiki/Acute_respiratory_distress "Acute respiratory distress"). The condition is caused by severe congestion of multiple organs by fluid that is inadequately circulated by the failing heart. An attack of [decompensation](/wiki/Decompensation "Decompensation") can be caused by underlying medical illness, such as [myocardial infarction](/wiki/Myocardial_infarction "Myocardial infarction"), an [abnormal heart rhythm](/wiki/Heart_arrhythmia "Heart arrhythmia"), [infection](/wiki/Infection "Infection"), or [thyroid disease](/wiki/Thyroid_disease "Thyroid disease").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Acute heart failure](/w/index.php?title=Acute_heart_failure&redirect=no "Acute heart failure"))

Medical condition

| Heart failure | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Acute interstitial pulmonary edema. Note enlarged heart size, apical vascular redistribution ( circle ), and small bilateral pleural effusions ( arrow ). | |
| [Specialty](/wiki/Medical_specialty "Medical specialty") | [Cardiology](/wiki/Cardiology "Cardiology") [Edit this on Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q4677930?uselang=en#P1995 "Edit this on Wikidata") |

**Acute decompensated heart failure** (**ADHF**) is a sudden worsening of the signs and symptoms of [heart failure](/wiki/Heart_failure "Heart failure"), which typically includes difficulty breathing ([dyspnea](/wiki/Dyspnea "Dyspnea")), [leg or feet swelling](/wiki/Edema "Edema"), and [fatigue](/wiki/Fatigue_(medical) "Fatigue (medical)").[[1]](#cite_note-1) ADHF is a common and potentially serious cause of [acute respiratory distress](/wiki/Acute_respiratory_distress "Acute respiratory distress"). The condition is caused by severe congestion of multiple organs by fluid that is inadequately circulated by the failing heart. An attack of [decompensation](/wiki/Decompensation "Decompensation") can be caused by underlying medical illness, such as [myocardial infarction](/wiki/Myocardial_infarction "Myocardial infarction"), an [abnormal heart rhythm](/wiki/Heart_arrhythmia "Heart arrhythmia"), [infection](/wiki/Infection "Infection"), or [thyroid disease](/wiki/Thyroid_disease "Thyroid disease").

## Acute Heart Failure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_decompensated_heart_failure&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Acute Heart Failure")]

Heart failure or cardiovascular insufficiency can be acute without being decompensated from a chronic condition. In this case, the signs of congestion such as weight gain and edema will not yet have developed. This is commonly due to pump failure or cardiovascular insufficiency after  [myocardial infarction](/wiki/Acute_myocardial_infarction "Acute myocardial infarction") when a significant loss of cardiac function occurs. Such patients will, have [shortness of breath](/wiki/Shortness_of_breath "Shortness of breath") due to poor tissue perfusion, tissue hypoxia and [metabolic acidosis](/wiki/Metabolic_acidosis "Metabolic acidosis").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Signs and symptoms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_decompensated_heart_failure&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Signs and symptoms")]

Cardiac symptoms of heart failure include chest pain/pressure and [palpitations](/wiki/Palpitation "Palpitation"). Common noncardiac signs and symptoms of heart failure include [loss of appetite](/wiki/Anorexia_(symptom) "Anorexia (symptom)"), nausea, weight loss, bloating, fatigue, weakness, [low urine output](/wiki/Oliguria "Oliguria"), [waking up at night to urinate](/wiki/Nocturia "Nocturia"), and cerebral symptoms of varying severity, ranging from anxiety to memory impairment and confusion.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Flash Pulmonary Edema

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_decompensated_heart_failure&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Flash Pulmonary Edema")]

Flash Pulmonary Edema or Crash Pulmonary Edema is a clinical characterization of acute heart failure with a dramatic presentation.[[4]](#cite_note-4) It is an acute cardiac disease precipitated by cardiac events and usually associated with severe hypertension.

## Causes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_decompensated_heart_failure&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Causes")]

Chronic stable [heart failure](/wiki/Heart_failure "Heart failure") may easily [decompensate](/wiki/Decompensation "Decompensation"). This most commonly results from an intercurrent illness (such as [pneumonia](/wiki/Pneumonia "Pneumonia")), [myocardial infarction](/wiki/Myocardial_infarction "Myocardial infarction") (a heart attack), [abnormal heart rhythms](/wiki/Cardiac_arrhythmia "Cardiac arrhythmia") (such as [atrial fibrillation](/wiki/Atrial_fibrillation "Atrial fibrillation")), uncontrolled [high blood pressure](/wiki/Hypertension "Hypertension"), or the person's failure to maintain a fluid restriction, diet, or medication.[[5]](#cite_note-OPTIMIZE-HF-5) Other well recognized precipitating factors include [anemia](/wiki/Anemia "Anemia") and [hyperthyroidism](/wiki/Hyperthyroidism "Hyperthyroidism") which place additional strain on the heart muscle. Excessive fluid or salt intake, and medication that causes fluid retention such as [NSAIDs](/wiki/Non-steroidal_anti-inflammatory_drug "Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug") and [thiazolidinediones](/wiki/Thiazolidinedione "Thiazolidinedione"), may also precipitate decompensation.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Diagnosis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_decompensated_heart_failure&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Diagnosis")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Elevated_JVP.JPG/250px-Elevated_JVP.JPG)](/wiki/File:Elevated_JVP.JPG)

A man with [congestive heart failure](/wiki/Congestive_heart_failure "Congestive heart failure") and marked jugular venous distension. External jugular vein marked by an arrow.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Combinpedal.jpg/250px-Combinpedal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Combinpedal.jpg)

Pitting edema during and after the application of pressure to the skin.

A [jugular venous distension](/wiki/Jugular_venous_distension "Jugular venous distension") is the most sensitive clinical sign for acute decompensation.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Treatment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_decompensated_heart_failure&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Treatment")]

In acute decompensated heart failure, the immediate goal is to re-establish adequate perfusion and oxygen delivery to end organs. This entails ensuring that [airway, breathing, and circulation](/wiki/ABC_(medicine) "ABC (medicine)") are adequate. Management consists of propping up the head of the patient, giving oxygen to correct hypoxemia, administering morphine, diuretics like [furosemide](/wiki/Furosemide "Furosemide"), addition of an ACE inhibitor, use of nitrates and use of [digoxin](/wiki/Digoxin "Digoxin") if indicated for the heart failure and if arrhythmic.[[8]](#cite_note-HFS2006-8)

### Oxygen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_decompensated_heart_failure&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Oxygen")]

Supplemental oxygen may be administered if [blood levels of oxygen are low](/wiki/Hypoxemia "Hypoxemia"); the Heart Failure Society of America, however, has recommended that it not be used routinely.[[8]](#cite_note-HFS2006-8)

### Medication

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_decompensated_heart_failure&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Medication")]

Initial therapy of acute decompensated heart failure usually includes some combination of a vasodilator such as nitroglycerin, a [loop diuretic](/wiki/Loop_diuretic "Loop diuretic") such as [furosemide](/wiki/Furosemide "Furosemide"), and [non-invasive positive pressure ventilation](/wiki/Mechanical_ventilation#Non-invasive_ventilation_(Non-invasive_Positive_Pressure_Ventilation_or_NIPPV) "Mechanical ventilation") (NIPPV).[[9]](#cite_note-Wakai_2013-9)

A number of different medications are required for people who are experiencing heart failure. Common types of medications that are prescribed for heart failure patients include [ACE inhibitors](/wiki/ACE_inhibitor "ACE inhibitor"), [vasodilators](/wiki/Vasodilation "Vasodilation"), [beta blockers](/wiki/Beta_blocker "Beta blocker"), [aspirin](/wiki/Aspirin "Aspirin"), [calcium channel blockers](/wiki/Calcium_channel_blocker "Calcium channel blocker"), and [cholesterol](/wiki/Cholesterol "Cholesterol") lowering medications such as [statins](/wiki/Statin "Statin"). Depending on the type of damage a patient has suffered and the underlying cause of the heart failure, any of these drug classes or a combination of them can be prescribed. Patients with heart pumping problems will use a different medication combination than those who are experiencing problems with the heart's ability to fill properly during [diastole](/wiki/Diastole "Diastole"). Potentially dangerous drug interactions can occur when different drugs mix together and work against each other.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

#### Vasodilators

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_decompensated_heart_failure&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Vasodilators")]

Nitrates such as [nitroglycerin](/wiki/Nitroglycerin "Nitroglycerin") ([glyceryl trinitrate](/wiki/Glyceryl_trinitrate "Glyceryl trinitrate")) and [isosorbide dinitrate](/wiki/Isosorbide_dinitrate "Isosorbide dinitrate") are often used as part of the initial therapy for ADHF.[[9]](#cite_note-Wakai_2013-9)

Another option is [nesiritide](/wiki/Nesiritide "Nesiritide"), although it should only be considered if conventional therapy has been ineffective or contraindicated as it is much more expensive than nitroglycerin and has not been shown to be of any greater benefit.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

A 2013 Cochrane review, compared nitrates and other pharmacological, non-pharmacological, and placebo interventions.[[9]](#cite_note-Wakai_2013-9) The review found no significant difference between the interventions in terms of symptom control or haemodynamic stability.[[9]](#cite_note-Wakai_2013-9)

The [National Institutes for Health and Care Excellence](/wiki/National_Institute_for_Health_and_Care_Excellence "National Institute for Health and Care Excellence") (NICE) guidelines do not recommend routinely offering nitrates in acute heart failure.[[12]](#cite_note-www.nice.org.uk_2014-12)

#### Diuretics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_decompensated_heart_failure&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Diuretics")]

Heart failure is usually associated with a volume overloaded state. Therefore, those with evidence of fluid overload should be treated initially with intravenous loop diuretics. In the absence of symptomatic low blood pressure intravenous nitroglycerin is often used in addition to diuretic therapy to improve congestive symptoms.[[8]](#cite_note-HFS2006-8)

Volume status should still be adequately evaluated. Some heart failure patients on chronic diuretics can undergo excessive diuresis. In the case of diastolic dysfunction without systolic dysfunction, fluid resuscitation may, in fact, improve circulation by decreasing heart rate, which will allow the ventricles more time to fill. Even if the patient is edematous, fluid resuscitation may be the first line of treatment if the person's blood pressure is low. The person may, in fact, have too little fluid in their blood vessels, but if the low blood pressure is due to [cardiogenic shock](/wiki/Cardiogenic_shock "Cardiogenic shock"), the administration of additional fluid may worsen the heart failure and associated low blood pressure. If the person's circulatory volume is adequate but there is persistent evidence of inadequate end-organ perfusion, inotropes may be administered. In certain circumstances, a [left ventricular assist device](/wiki/Ventricular_assist_device "Ventricular assist device") (LVAD) may be necessary.

Once the person is stabilized, attention can be turned to treating pulmonary edema to improve oxygenation. Intravenous furosemide is generally the first line. However, people on long-standing diuretic regimens can become tolerant, and dosages must be progressively increased. If high doses of furosemide are inadequate, boluses or continuous infusions of bumetanide may be preferred. These [loop diuretics](/wiki/Loop_diuretics "Loop diuretics") may be combined with [thiazide diuretics](/wiki/Thiazide_diuretics "Thiazide diuretics") such as oral [metolazone](/wiki/Metolazone "Metolazone") or intravenous [chlorothiazide](/wiki/Chlorothiazide "Chlorothiazide") for a synergistic effect. Intravenous preparations are physiologically preferred because of more predictable absorption due to intestinal edema, however, oral preparations can be significantly more cost effective.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

#### Others

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_decompensated_heart_failure&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Others")]

ACE inhibitors and ARBs

The effectiveness and safety of [ACE inhibitors](/wiki/ACE_inhibitor "ACE inhibitor") and [angiotensin receptor blockers](/wiki/Angiotensin_receptor_blockers "Angiotensin receptor blockers") (ARBs) acutely in ADHF have not been well studied, but are potentially harmful. A person should be stabilized before therapy with either of these medication classes is initiated.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Individuals with poor kidney perfusion are especially at risk for kidney impairment inherent with these medications.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Beta-blockers

[Beta-blockers](/wiki/Beta-blockers "Beta-blockers") are stopped or decreased in people with acutely decompensated heart failure and a low blood pressure. However, continuation of beta-blockers may be appropriate if the blood pressure is adequate.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Inotropic agents

[Inotropes](/wiki/Inotrope "Inotrope") are indicated if low blood pressure ( [SBP](/wiki/Systolic_blood_pressure "Systolic blood pressure") < 90 mmHg ) is present.

The [National Institutes for Health and Care Excellence](/wiki/National_Institute_for_Health_and_Care_Excellence "National Institute for Health and Care Excellence") (NICE) guidelines do not recommend routinely offering inotropes in acute heart failure.[[12]](#cite_note-www.nice.org.uk_2014-12) However, they recommend it be considered in patients with ADHF and potentially reversible cardiogenic shock.[[12]](#cite_note-www.nice.org.uk_2014-12)

Opioids

[Opioids](/wiki/Opioids "Opioids") have traditionally been used in the treatment of the [acute pulmonary edema](/wiki/Acute_pulmonary_edema "Acute pulmonary edema") that results from acute decompensated heart failure. A 2006 review, however, found little evidence to support this practice.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The [National Institutes for Health and Care Excellence](/wiki/National_Institute_for_Health_and_Care_Excellence "National Institute for Health and Care Excellence") (NICE) guidelines do not recommend routinely offering opioids in acute heart failure.[[12]](#cite_note-www.nice.org.uk_2014-12)

### Ventilation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_decompensated_heart_failure&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Ventilation")]

[Continuous positive airway pressure](/wiki/Positive_airway_pressure "Positive airway pressure") may be applied using a face mask; this has been shown to improve symptoms more quickly than oxygen therapy alone,[[18]](#cite_note-18) and has been shown to reduce the risk of death.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) Severe [respiratory failure](/wiki/Respiratory_failure "Respiratory failure") requires treatment with [endotracheal intubation](/wiki/Endotracheal_intubation "Endotracheal intubation") and [mechanical ventilation](/wiki/Mechanical_ventilation "Mechanical ventilation").

### Ultrafiltration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_decompensated_heart_failure&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Ultrafiltration")]

[Ultrafiltration](/wiki/Ultrafiltration "Ultrafiltration") can be used to remove fluids in people with ADHF associated with [kidney failure](/wiki/Kidney_failure "Kidney failure"). Studies have found that it decreases health care utilization at 90 days.[[21]](#cite_note-21)
One such method is [aquapheresis ultra-filtration](/wiki/Aquapheresis "Aquapheresis")

A 2022 Cochrane review on the benefits, efficacy, and safety of ultrafiltration compared to diuretic therapy found that ultrafiltration probably reduces the incidence of heart-failure related hospitalisation in the long term.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The [National Institutes for Health and Care Excellence](/wiki/National_Institute_for_Health_and_Care_Excellence "National Institute for Health and Care Excellence") (NICE) guidelines do not recommend routinely offering ultrafiltration in acute heart failure.[[12]](#cite_note-www.nice.org.uk_2014-12)

### Surgery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_decompensated_heart_failure&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Surgery")]

In some cases, doctors recommend [surgery](/wiki/Surgery "Surgery") to treat the underlying problem that led to heart failure.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Different procedures are available depending on the level of necessity and include [coronary artery bypass surgery](/wiki/Coronary_artery_bypass_surgery "Coronary artery bypass surgery"), [heart valve](/wiki/Heart_valve "Heart valve") repair or replacement, or [heart transplantation](/wiki/Heart_transplantation "Heart transplantation"). During these procedures, devices such as heart pumps, [pacemakers](/wiki/Artificial_pacemaker "Artificial pacemaker"), or [defibrillators](/wiki/Defibrillation "Defibrillation") might be implanted. The treatment of heart disease is rapidly changing and thus new therapies for acute heart failure treatment are being introduced to save more lives from these massive attacks.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

[Bypass surgery](/wiki/Bypass_surgery "Bypass surgery") is performed by removing a vein from the arm or leg, or an artery from the chest and replacing the blocked artery in the heart. This allows the blood to flow more freely through the heart. Valve repair is where the valve that is causing heart failure is modified by removing excess valve tissues that cause them to close too tightly. In some cases, [annuloplasty](/wiki/Intervertebral_disc_annuloplasty "Intervertebral disc annuloplasty") is required to replace the ring around the valves. If the repair of the valve is not possible, it is replaced by an [artificial heart valve](/wiki/Artificial_heart_valve "Artificial heart valve"). The final step is heart replacement. When severe heart failure is present and medicines or other heart procedures are not effective, the diseased heart needs to be replaced.

Another common procedure used to treat heart failure patients is an [angioplasty](/wiki/Angioplasty "Angioplasty"). Is a procedure used to improve the symptoms of [coronary artery disease](/wiki/Coronary_artery_disease "Coronary artery disease") (CAD), reduce the damage to the heart muscle after a [heart attack](/wiki/Myocardial_infarction "Myocardial infarction"), and reduce the risk of death in some patients.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

A [pacemaker](/wiki/Pacemaker "Pacemaker") is a small device that's placed in the chest or abdomen to help control abnormal heart rhythms.[[26]](#cite_note-26) They work by sending electric pulses to the heart to prompt it to beat at a rate that is considered to be normal and are used to treat patients with arrhythmias. They can be used to treat hearts that are classified as either a [tachycardia](/wiki/Tachycardia "Tachycardia") that beats too fast, or a [bradycardia](/wiki/Bradycardia "Bradycardia") that beats too slow.
